monsters.name: Alienoid
monsters.tagline: The Space Challenger. Turns every disadvantage into fuel.
monsters.content_html:
Overview

Nothing goes to waste. Damage converts to energy, the card market becomes a tool, and every bad situation becomes an opportunity.

As an Intermediate monster with a Harvester and Collector playstyle, Alienoid rewards patience and board reading over brute force.

Playstyle

The evolution cards define this clearly: Anger Batteries converts incoming damage directly into energy, Superior Alien Technology makes expensive cards affordable, and Adapting Technology reshapes the market on demand.

The more pressure opponents apply, the more fuel Alienoid generates. The core loop is: absorb hits, convert to energy, buy powerful cards, escalate.

Evolution Cards

Alienoid's eight evolution cards reward adaptation and energy management. You start with one in hand and draw more by rolling three or more hearts.

Superior Alien Technology (Permanent)

Buy KEEP cards at half cost (rounded up). When you do, place a UFO token on the card. At the start of your turn, roll a die for each KEEP card with a UFO token. Discard any card that rolls a CLAW. Maximum 3 UFO-tokened cards at a time.

Alienoid's most explosive card. Grabbing a high cost card for half price is potentially game-winning, but the discard risk is real. Prioritise cards that give immediate value rather than long-term engine pieces that need time to pay off.

Mothership Support (Permanent)

Once per turn, spend 1 ENERGY to gain 1 HEART.

Converts energy directly into survivability. Lets you stay in Tokyo longer than opponents expect and keeps your health buffer stable without relying on heart dice.

Exotic Arms (Permanent)

Before you roll, place 2 ENERGY on this card. If you roll at least 3 CLAWS, take the energy back and deal 2 extra damage to each monster you wound. If you don't, lose the 2 ENERGY and lose 2 HEART.

High risk, high reward. Only gamble with this when you have energy to spare. The downside is punishing.

Funny Looking But Dangerous (Permanent)

If you roll at least three 2s, every other monster loses 1 HEART.

Passive and consistent once active. Rolling three 2s happens more often than expected and the chip damage accumulates quickly across the table.

Adapting Technology (Permanent)

Comes with 3 ALIEN tokens. On your turn, remove a token to discard the 3 face-up Power cards and replace them with 3 new ones. Discarded when all tokens are used.

A controlled market reset. Best saved for when the market is dead or when an opponent is clearly eyeing a card you want to deny them.

Anger Batteries (Temporary)

Gain 1 ENERGY for each HEART you lost this turn.

Alienoid's most on-brand card. Converts punishment directly into fuel. Play it after a heavy damage turn to refill your engine entirely.

Precision Field Support (Temporary)

Draw Power cards until you reveal a KEEP card costing 4 ENERGY or less. Take it for free and discard the rest.

A free card from the deck. Best used when the face-up market has nothing worth buying.

Alien Scourge (Temporary)

Gain 2 STAR.

Hold it until you are 2 VP from winning and close out cleanly.

Tips & Strategies

Early Game

  • Prioritise lightning bolts over everything else. The entire kit runs on energy.
  • Your opening evolution sets the gameplan. Mothership Support means lean into sustain. Superior Alien Technology means pivot to card buying immediately.

Late Game

  • Hold Alien Scourge until you are exactly 2 VP from winning.
  • Exotic Arms is safer to gamble with once you have a large energy buffer built up.

Best Evolution Combos

  • Anger Batteries + Exotic Arms — take damage, refuel, then bet the energy on a big claw turn.
  • Superior Alien Technology + Precision Field Support — half-price buys and a free card in the same round.
  • Mothership Support + Funny Looking But Dangerous — heal passively while dealing chip damage every turn.

Common Mistakes

  • Gambling Exotic Arms when low on health or energy, the penalty hits both
  • Using all Adapting Technology tokens too early before opponents have stockpiled energy
  • Playing Anger Batteries before taking damage that turn, it needs to be reactive
  • Rolling claws when lightning bolts are almost always the better choice

monsters.name: Cyber Kitty
monsters.tagline: Nine lives, zero mercy. The cat always lands on her feet.
monsters.content_html:
Overview

Chaos incarnate. Fragile, unpredictable, and capable of swinging a game completely in a single turn. The evolutions punish opponents in Tokyo, steal resources directly, and culminate in one of the most dramatic comeback mechanics in the game.

Rated Advanced with a Disruptor and Smasher playstyle, Cyber Kitty rewards players who enjoy high-variance, high-reward gameplay and aren't afraid of risky turns.

Playstyle

Two things: rolling ones and stealing resources. Meow Missile and Mouse Hunter both trigger off a single 1 in your final roll, turning a normally wasted die face into a free claw and a free VP simultaneously. Play With Your Food converts strong claw turns into direct resource theft from Tokyo occupants.

Nine Lives means elimination isn't the end. The core loop is: roll ones to amplify, steal resources, hit Tokyo hard, trigger Nine Lives, win from nowhere.

Evolution Cards

Cyber Kitty's eight evolution cards build a cohesive kit around rolling ones, stealing resources, and surviving situations that would eliminate any other monster.

Meow Missile (Permanent)

If you roll at least one 1, add a CLAW to your roll.

A passive damage amplifier. Most players reroll ones away out of habit. With this active, a single 1 in your final roll means a free claw on top of everything else. Stack with Mouse Hunter for turns where keeping ones is the primary goal.

Mouse Hunter (Permanent)

If you roll at least one 1, gain 1 STAR.

A free VP every turn you end with a 1 in your roll. Combined with Meow Missile, a single 1 simultaneously gives a free claw and a free VP. Keeping one die showing a 1 becomes a legitimate strategy rather than a concession.

Feline Motor (Permanent)

During other monsters' movement phases, if Tokyo is empty, you can take control of it instead of the monster whose turn it is.

Tokyo control that costs nothing on your own turn. If anyone yields and Tokyo empties during their turn, slide in before the active monster gets there. Opponents can't account for it because it triggers on their turn, not yours.

Play With Your Food (Permanent)

When a monster in Tokyo must lose at least 2 HEART from your CLAWS, you can make them lose 2 HEART fewer and instead steal 1 STAR and 1 ENERGY from them directly.

Turns every strong claw turn into a choice. Deal full damage, or steal a star and energy from the Tokyo occupant? Against energy-rich monsters the theft is often worth more than the extra damage.

Cat Nip (Temporary)

Play at the start of your turn. You only have one roll this turn, but double the result.

The highest ceiling single-turn card in the kit. Six dice become effectively twelve. Play it on a turn where the first roll is already strong. A mediocre roll doubled is still mediocre.

Electro-Scratch (Temporary)

All other monsters lose 1 HEART.

A board-wide ping that hits everyone simultaneously. Use it to finish off a weakened monster, pressure the table, or soften up a Tokyo occupant before a claw turn.

Mega Purr (Temporary)

All other monsters give you 1 ENERGY or 1 STAR (they choose).

Mandatory tribute from the entire table. Every opponent must hand over either energy or a VP with no option to give nothing. Best played when opponents are energy-rich and star-rich so there is no easy choice.

Nine Lives (Temporary)

If you reach 0 HEART, discard all your cards including evolutions, lose all energy and stars, and leave Tokyo. Gain 9 HEART and 9 STAR, and continue playing.

The most dramatic evolution card in the game. Instead of being eliminated, reset to 9 health and 9 VP instantly. The cost is severe, every card and resource gone, but from 9 VP two good turns can win the game. Hold it as long as possible and let opponents think they can eliminate you.

Tips & Strategies

Early Game

  • Get Meow Missile and Mouse Hunter active together as fast as possible. A single 1 in your roll then generates a free claw and a free VP every turn.
  • Keep Nine Lives in hand from the moment you draw it. Its value comes entirely from being a surprise.

Late Game

  • Trigger Nine Lives at exactly 0 health, ideally when opponents are about to pile on. The instant 9 VP reset can flip the entire game.
  • Cat Nip is the best closer when health is stable. Doubling a strong natural roll can end the game in a single turn.

Best Evolution Combos

  • Meow Missile + Mouse Hunter + Cat Nip — double your dice with both one-triggers active for a turn that generates VP, claws, and raw dice value simultaneously.
  • Play With Your Food + Feline Motor — steal resources from Tokyo occupants with claws, then slide into Tokyo the moment they yield.
  • Nine Lives + Electro-Scratch or Mega Purr — after triggering Nine Lives at 9 VP, immediately play a resource grab or board ping to accelerate the closing push.

Common Mistakes

  • Rerolling ones away out of habit, both Meow Missile and Mouse Hunter need at least one 1 to trigger
  • Playing Nine Lives when health is merely low, save it for the moment you actually hit 0
  • Using Cat Nip on a mediocre first roll, doubling a bad roll is still a bad roll
  • Ignoring Feline Motor once active, track Tokyo's status during other players' turns
  • Using Mega Purr when opponents are resource-poor, it needs a full table to be worth play

monsters.name: Gigazaur
monsters.tagline: Raw nuclear power. Built to smash, not finesse.
monsters.content_html:
Overview

Gigazaur is a nuclear-powered wrecking machine with one goal: deal damage. The playstyle revolves around maximising claw output, keeping opponents on the back foot, and turning Tokyo into a warzone nobody else wants to enter.

As a Beginner monster with a Smasher, Scorer, and Climber playstyle, Gigazaur is straightforward to pick up and rewarding to master.

Playstyle

Gigazaur is a Brawler. The evolution cards make this clear: Gamma Breath guarantees a free claw every turn, Defender of Tokyo drains every opponent's VP just for staying in Tokyo, and Heat Vision punishes anyone who dares hit back. Subtlety is not part of the gameplan.

The core loop is: roll claws, deal damage, occupy Tokyo, drain VP.

Evolution Cards

Gigazaur's eight evolution cards turn raw dice rolls into precision tools and punish opponents for engaging.

Tail Sweep (Permanent)

Once per turn, change one of your rolled dice to a 1 or 2.

A flexible utility card. Use it to complete a three-of-a-kind number combo you just missed, or fish for VP points when close to winning. Pairs well with Defender of Tokyo for squeezing extra value out of number dice.

Gamma Breath (Permanent)

Once per turn, change one of your rolled dice to a CLAW.

Gigazaur's most important evolution. Guarantees at least one claw every turn making damage output completely reliable. Stack with natural claw rolls for consistently high damage turns.

Heat Vision (Permanent)

Monsters that wound you with CLAWS lose 1 STAR.

A passive deterrent that punishes aggression. Opponents thinking about attacking will have to weigh losing VP, which can meaningfully slow down anyone racing for points.

Defender of Tokyo (Permanent)

If you start your turn in Tokyo, all other monsters lose 1 STAR.

Gigazaur's most powerful evolution. Turning Tokyo occupancy into a VP drain every turn is devastating. Combine with Tail Sweep to keep rolling number dice while this bleeds everyone dry.

Saurian Adaptability (Temporary)

Choose a die face. Take all dice showing that face and flip them all to a single face of your choice.

An incredible tempo card. Convert a bad roll into a focused high-value turn instantly — flip a pile of lightning bolts into claws or a clean three-of-a-kind.

Primal Bellow (Temporary)

All other monsters lose 2 STAR.

Strips VP from every opponent at once. Save it for when multiple opponents are close to 20 VP, or use it as a closer to stretch the gap when already leading.

Radioactive Waste (Temporary)

Gain 2 ENERGY and 1 HEART.

Gigazaur doesn't usually care about energy, but this freebie opens up cards like Nova Breath or Extra Head. The bonus heart is a small but welcome top-up.

Detachable Tail (Temporary)

You cannot lose HEART this turn.

A get-out-of-jail card. Use it on a turn where heavy incoming damage is expected, or hold it as insurance when health is low. Pairs perfectly with Defender of Tokyo — stay in Tokyo, drain VP, take zero damage.

Tips & Strategies

Early Game

  • Roll as many claws as possible in the first two rounds. Health is a resource to spend, not hoard.
  • Get Gamma Breath active as fast as possible. A free claw every turn makes damage output completely consistent.

Late Game

  • Commit fully to eliminating a wounded monster. Partial damage just lets them heal.
  • Hold Primal Bellow and Detachable Tail for the final push.

Best Evolution Combos

  • Gamma Breath + Tail Sweep — guaranteed claw plus flexible number conversion every turn. Consistent dice control.
  • Defender of Tokyo + Detachable Tail — stay in Tokyo, drain VP, take zero damage that turn.
  • Saurian Adaptability + Defender of Tokyo — convert a bad roll into a VP dice turn while the Tokyo passive bleeds opponents.

Card Priorities

  1. Nova Breath — hits everyone outside Tokyo, pairs perfectly with aggression
  2. Extra Head — doubles claw output
  3. Complete Destruction — closes out the board when ready

Common Mistakes

  • Spreading damage across multiple targets instead of focusing one down
  • Ignoring VP entirely and letting a camper quietly hit 20
  • Sitting on Detachable Tail too long, use it proactively when staying in Tokyo
  • Underestimating Defender of Tokyo, the VP drain is often more powerful than the damage output

monsters.name: Meka Dragon
monsters.content_html:
Overview

Meka Dragon is the card-game player's monster. While others are rolling dice and punching things, energy is accumulating and converting into powerful combos that snowball out of control. The ceiling is much higher than it first appears, and the evolution cards turn the whole kit into something genuinely terrifying once they stack up.

As an Intermediate monster with a Smasher playstyle, Meka Dragon rewards patience and board reading over brute force.

Playstyle

Meka Dragon evolutions tell the full story: Target Acquired marks whoever hits you hardest and amplifies every attack you land on them, Lightning Armor reflects damage back at attackers on a dice roll, and Breath of Doom turns every Tokyo hit into board-wide collateral damage. The more opponents engage, the more dangerous things get.

The core loop is: build energy, mark targets, amplify damage, punish aggression.

Evolution Cards

Meka Dragon's eight evolution cards create a web of damage amplification and retribution. The more opponents attack, the more dangerous the kit becomes.

Target Acquired (Permanent)

When a monster makes you lose HEART with CLAWS, you can give them the TARGET token. That monster loses 1 extra HEART every time you deal damage to them.

A slow-burn punisher. Mark whoever hits hardest and every subsequent attack on them deals bonus damage. Pairs exceptionally well with Claws of Steel and Mecha Blast.

Claws of Steel (Permanent)

On your turn, if you make another monster lose at least 3 HEART, they lose 1 extra HEART.

A passive damage amplifier that rewards going big. Rolling three or more claws already puts opponents under pressure. Stack with Target Acquired to punish anyone who's been marked.

Lightning Armor (Permanent)

When you lose HEART, roll a die for each HEART lost. For each CLAW rolled, the monster whose turn it is loses 1 HEART.

One of the most disruptive evolutions in the game. Opponents attacking have to gamble that their claw dice won't bounce straight back at them. In Tokyo this can reflect serious damage back across the table.

Breath of Doom (Permanent)

When you make monsters in Tokyo lose at least 1 HEART, monsters outside Tokyo also lose 1 HEART (except you).

A board-wide pressure tool. Hitting Tokyo now pings every monster not in Tokyo as collateral damage. Combined with Claws of Steel a single claw turn can damage every monster on the board simultaneously.

Tune-Up (Temporary)

Play before rolling dice. If you are not in Tokyo, skip your turn, gain 4 HEART and 2 ENERGY.

A deliberate trade — forfeit your turn entirely to fully restock. Best used when health is dangerously low and the energy engine has stalled. Never use it while in Tokyo, it only triggers outside.

Programmed to Destroy (Permanent)

Gain 3 STAR and 2 ENERGY each time a monster's health reaches 0.

A win condition accelerator. Each elimination pays out 3 VP and 2 energy, enough to buy another card or fuel a follow-up turn. Turns kills into a snowballing resource engine.

Destructive Analysis (Temporary)

Gain 1 ENERGY for each CLAW you rolled this turn.

The bridge between aggression and the energy engine. A turn with four claws suddenly also generates four energy. Best used after dealing damage to immediately convert momentum into resources.

Mecha Blast (Temporary)

Each monster you make lose HEART with your CLAWS loses 2 extra HEART.

A massive burst damage card. Every monster hit this turn takes 2 bonus damage. Stack with Target Acquired and Claws of Steel for a single turn that can cripple multiple opponents. Save it for when targets are already weakened.

Tips & Strategies

Early Game

  • Roll lightning bolts almost exclusively for the first few turns. The whole kit runs on energy.
  • Get Target Acquired active early and mark the most aggressive player immediately.

Late Game

  • Programmed to Destroy makes finishing off weakened monsters a priority. Each kill pays 3 VP and 2 energy.
  • Hold Mecha Blast for a turn where multiple opponents are low. The 2 bonus damage per target is devastating at that point.

Best Evolution Combos

  • Target Acquired + Claws of Steel + Mecha Blast — mark a target, guarantee the threshold bonus, then play Mecha Blast for a turn that deals massive bonus damage to the marked target.
  • Breath of Doom + Claws of Steel — hit Tokyo hard enough to trigger the threshold and every outside monster takes 1 damage too.
  • Lightning Armor + Programmed to Destroy — sit in Tokyo, reflect damage back, collect 3 VP and 2 energy per kill.
  • Destructive Analysis + any high-claw turn — convert aggression directly into energy then spend it immediately.

Card Priorities

  1. Apartment Building — converts energy advantage into VP efficiently
  2. Nova Breath — stacks with Breath of Doom for extra board pressure
  3. Acid Attack — persistent damage that compounds over time
  4. Eater of the Dead — pairs with Programmed to Destroy in attrition games

Common Mistakes

  • Buying expensive cards before the energy engine is established
  • Entering Tokyo too early, getting hit in setup turns stalls the engine before it starts
  • Forgetting to actually win, energy and evolutions are a means not an end
  • Wasting Mecha Blast on a single target, it shines when hitting multiple monsters at once
  • Not marking a target with Target Acquired early enough, every hit taken without marking is a missed opportunity
  • Using Tune-Up while in Tokyo, it only works outside

monsters.name: Space Penguin
monsters.tagline: The Antarctic Anarchist. Steals your tools, freezes your plans.
monsters.content_html:
Overview

Space Penguin is the most disruptive monster in the game. Winning comes not from dealing the most damage or sitting in Tokyo the longest, but from dismantling everyone else's strategy while quietly building an advantage. Evolutions copy other monsters' cards, lock dice, strip rerolls, and can freeze the entire board mid-game.

Rated Advanced with a Disruptor and Collector playstyle, Space Penguin rewards players who pay close attention to other monsters' boards. If you don't know what other evolutions do, most of the kit loses its teeth.

Playstyle

Space Penguin is a Disruptor with no fixed gameplan. The evolutions define this completely: Icy Reflection copies whatever the strongest evolution on the board is, Blizzard strips all rerolls and locks the Tokyo occupant in place, and Cold Wave costs every opponent a die for a full round. The strategy reshapes itself around whatever the table is doing.

The core loop is: observe what's working, copy or counter it, buy time while opponents stall, close out with VP.

Evolution Cards

Space Penguin's eight evolution cards are among the most unique in the game. Several interact directly with other players' cards and dice in ways no other monster can.

Icy Reflection (Permanent)

Choose an evolution card in front of another monster and place a Snowflake token on it. Icy Reflection becomes a copy of that card. If the copied card is removed from play, Icy Reflection is discarded.

The most defining card in the kit. Instead of building an engine, steal someone else's. The card is only as strong as what's available to copy, which is why knowing other monsters' evolutions is essential. Watch the linked discard — if the original card leaves play, so does this one.

Black Diamond (Permanent)

Gain 1 extra STAR each time you take control of Tokyo or choose to stay when you could have yielded.

A VP engine that rewards decisiveness. Every active Tokyo hold banks a bonus star. Doesn't require camping — quick in-and-out grabs generate consistent VP. Pairs well with Cold Wave and Blizzard since limiting opponent dice makes staying in Tokyo much safer.

Freeze Ray (Permanent)

When you wound a monster in Tokyo, give them this card. At the start of their turn they choose a die face — that face has no effect this turn. You take the card back at the end of their turn.

A recurring debuff that bounces between you and the Tokyo occupant. Each time you hit the Tokyo monster, they lose a die face for their next turn. Particularly strong against energy-dependent monsters who rely on lightning bolt turns.

Encased in Ice (Permanent)

Spend 1 ENERGY to choose one of your rolled dice. That die is frozen until the start of your next turn — it cannot be changed and is used normally by other monsters during the Resolve Dice phase.

Lock in a claw, a lightning bolt, or a number die before rerolling the rest, guaranteeing at least one result. Costs energy but pays off consistently as a precision tool.

Miraculous Catch (Permanent)

Once per turn during the Buy Power Cards phase, shuffle the discard pile and reveal one card randomly. Buy it for 1 ENERGY less than normal or discard it.

Access to cards everyone else already passed on, at a discount. Best used when the face-up market is weak or when a powerful card was recently discarded. Stacks well with energy generation to keep buying every turn.

Deep Dive (Temporary)

Look at the top 3 Power cards of the deck. Choose one and play it in front of you for free. Put the others on the bottom.

A free card with full information. Unlike Miraculous Catch this is deterministic. Use it when the face-up market has nothing useful or when a specific card type is needed to complete a combo.

Cold Wave (Temporary)

Until your next turn, all other monsters roll with 1 fewer die.

Board-wide disruption on a timer. Every opponent loses a die for a full round. Best played just before a round where opponents are likely to take aggressive or high-value turns. Stack with Blizzard in the same round for total dice lockdown.

Blizzard (Temporary)

Play during your turn. Until the start of your next turn, monsters only have a single roll and cannot yield Tokyo.

The most powerful disruption card in the kit. Strips all rerolls from every opponent for a full round and locks the Tokyo occupant in place. Use it when a Tokyo occupant is low on health, or when opponents are sitting on powerful dice combinations they were planning to reroll into.

Tips & Strategies

Early Game

  • Scout other monsters' evolutions immediately. Icy Reflection is the most valuable card and its ceiling depends entirely on what's available to copy.
  • Roll lightning bolts early. The kit is energy-hungry and reserves are needed for Encased in Ice and Miraculous Catch.

Late Game

  • Blizzard is the best closer. Locking rerolls and forcing a Tokyo hold means a weak occupant can't escape punishment.
  • Watch for Miraculous Catch value when the discard pile is full of expensive cards others passed on.

Best Evolution Combos

  • Icy Reflection + any strong permanent — copying a top-tier evolution from another monster gives VP generation or damage output that was never earned independently.
  • Cold Wave + Blizzard — play both in the same round for a full dice lockdown. Opponents get one roll with one fewer die and no rerolls.
  • Freeze Ray + Encased in Ice — debuff the Tokyo occupant's dice while locking in your best result before rerolling.
  • Black Diamond + Blizzard — force the Tokyo occupant to stay put, then grab Tokyo for the bonus VP.

Card Priorities

  1. Freeze Time — stalling opponents while disruption compounds is extremely powerful
  2. Apartment Building — converts energy advantage into VP efficiently
  3. Herd Culler — cheap damage that keeps opponents' health low for Blizzard finishes
  4. Background Dweller — staying out of Tokyo safely while accumulating energy suits the patient gameplan

Common Mistakes

  • Using Icy Reflection on a weak evolution just because it's available, wait for something genuinely powerful
  • Playing Blizzard too early before multiple opponents are vulnerable, it's a one-shot
  • Ignoring energy generation, both Encased in Ice and Miraculous Catch run dry fast without lightning bolt investment
  • Forgetting that Freeze Ray bounces back, losing track of it means losing the recurring debuff
  • Treating Space Penguin like a damage dealer, VP comes from disruption and card value not claws
  • Using Cold Wave and Blizzard on separate turns when stacking them in the same round is far more effective

monsters.name: The King
monsters.tagline: King of the concrete jungle. Tokyo is his throne — and he intends to keep it.
monsters.content_html:
Overview

The King is a giant gorilla with one obsession: owning Tokyo. Every evolution card either rewards being in Tokyo, punishes opponents for trying to remove control, or reclaims the throne the moment it's lost. Energy and card engines are irrelevant. The throne is all that matters.

A strong pick for players who enjoy positional play and VP accumulation over raw aggression.

Playstyle

The King is a Defender and Scorer. The evolutions make the gameplan explicit: Twas Beauty Killed the Beast generates VP at the end of every monster's turn while in Tokyo, I Am the King! doubles the standard Tokyo VP rewards, and Monkey Rush reclaims Tokyo instantly the moment someone else leaves. Every card in the kit exists to hold Tokyo or punish those who challenge it.

The core loop is: get into Tokyo, stack VP from multiple sources simultaneously, never let anyone take it.

Evolution Cards

The King's eight evolution cards are almost entirely focused on Tokyo control and VP generation. Getting even two or three active simultaneously creates a VP engine that's very hard to race against.

Twas Beauty Killed the Beast (Permanent)

While in Tokyo, gain 1 STAR at the end of every monster's turn including yours. Discard this card and lose all your STAR if you leave Tokyo.

The most powerful and most dangerous evolution in the kit. This generates VP compounding fast each round. The catch is brutal: leaving Tokyo costs every star accumulated on this card. Never play it without enough health to absorb pressure and either Simian Scamper or Chest Thumping ready as insurance.

I Am the King! (Permanent)

Gain 1 extra STAR when you take control of Tokyo or start your turn there.

A straightforward VP amplifier. Taking Tokyo now gives 2 VP instead of 1, and starting a turn there gives 2 VP instead of 1. Low risk, permanent benefit. Get this out early and it pays off every single turn.

Alpha Male (Permanent)

If you wound at least one monster with CLAWS, gain 1 STAR.

Turns every claw turn into a VP gain regardless of Tokyo position. Opponents can't ignore The King just because control of Tokyo was yielded. Stack with I Am the King! for VP generation from multiple sources every turn.

Chest Thumping (Permanent)

You can force monsters you wound with CLAWS to yield Tokyo.

Control made absolute. Instead of waiting for an opponent to choose to yield, the decision is yours. Hit a Tokyo occupant hard enough and eject them immediately. Pairs perfectly with Twas Beauty Killed the Beast since getting back into Tokyo on demand protects the VP stack.

Giant Banana (Temporary)

Gain 2 HEART.

Simple sustain. Use it when health drops low enough that staying in Tokyo becomes risky. Two health can be the difference between riding out one more round of attacks and being forced to yield and losing everything on Twas Beauty Killed the Beast.

Jungle Frenzy (Temporary)

Play at the end of your movement phase, if you wounded the Tokyo controller with CLAWS but didn't take control. Take an extra turn.

A precision tempo card. If the Tokyo occupant was wounded but didn't yield, a full bonus turn triggers immediately. Use it to keep pressing, roll for VP, or set up the killing blow.

Simian Scamper (Temporary)

If you yield Tokyo, do not lose HEART. You can't lose HEART this turn.

The safety net for Twas Beauty Killed the Beast. If forced to yield, at least walking away at full health is guaranteed. Also useful as a general defensive card when opponents are stacking claw damage and survival is the priority.

Monkey Rush (Temporary)

Play when the monster controlling Tokyo leaves or is eliminated. Take control of Tokyo.

An instant Tokyo grab triggered by someone else's misfortune. Hold this in hand and one monster's bad luck is all it takes to reclaim the throne. Best played in response to a high-health monster being forced out before anyone else can react.

Tips & Strategies

Early Game

  • Get into Tokyo fast and establish I Am the King! first. Low risk and it pays off every single turn from that point.
  • Hold Monkey Rush in hand at all times until Tokyo needs to be reclaimed urgently.

Late Game

  • With Twas Beauty Killed the Beast and I Am the King! both active, VP is generating passively every round. Play defensively and let the clock win.
  • Jungle Frenzy is a strong closer. A bonus turn when near 20 VP can end the game immediately.

Best Evolution Combos

  • Twas Beauty Killed the Beast + I Am the King! + Alpha Male — VP from three sources every round. Opponents must respond immediately or lose the VP race entirely.
  • Chest Thumping + Monkey Rush — force anyone out of Tokyo with claws then instantly reclaim it. Near-permanent Tokyo control.
  • Twas Beauty Killed the Beast + Simian Scamper — accumulate VP safely in Tokyo with Simian Scamper as the escape hatch that costs no health on the way out.

Card Priorities

  1. Regeneration — keeps Tokyo stays longer and protects the VP stack
  2. Even Bigger — extra health extends Tokyo stays significantly
  3. Armor Plating — reduces incoming damage making it harder to force out
  4. Domination — closes out the VP race faster when already ahead

Common Mistakes

  • Activating Twas Beauty Killed the Beast too early without health insurance, losing all accumulated stars to a forced yield is catastrophic
  • Holding Monkey Rush too long and letting someone else grab Tokyo when it was needed
  • Ignoring Alpha Male, VP from claw turns outside Tokyo adds up faster than expected
  • Staying in Tokyo at 3-4 health hoping to survive one more round, know when to yield before being forced out
  • Treating Chest Thumping as reactive rather than proactive, use it to eject threats before they become dangerous
